description.summary: Luminous optical phenomena reported in the sky or near the ground before, during, or shortly after seismic activity, with reports spanning more than a millennium.
description.full: Earthquake lights (EQL) are anomalous luminous phenomena reported in the sky or near the ground in association with seismic activity, often described as glowing orbs, sheet-like flashes, blue-white discharges, flame-like columns, or aurora-like glows. Reports go back more than a thousand years (the 869 Jōgan earthquake in Japan is among the earliest written accounts) and continued sporadically through the 19th and 20th centuries before becoming photographically documented in the 1965–1967 Matsushiro swarm in Nagano, Japan. Modern scientific work distinguishes preseismic lights (occurring seconds to weeks before a major earthquake, generally close to the epicentre) from coseismic lights (occurring during seismic-wave passage, sometimes hundreds of kilometres away). The 2014 study by Robert Thériault and Friedemann Freund in 'Seismological Research Letters' compiled 65 documented cases since 1600 and proposed that the lights occur predominantly along subvertical faults in rifting environments. The leading mechanistic hypotheses involve mobilisation of positive holes (peroxy-bond defects in stressed igneous rock; Freund), piezoelectric generation in quartz-rich rocks, and triboelectric / triboluminescent processes (Shinbrot et al., 2014). Mainstream geophysics now broadly accepts that EQL exist as a real if poorly-understood phenomenon, while remaining cautious about specific reports.

etymology.origin: English compound term coined in mid-20th-century geophysical literature; Japanese parallel terms ('jishin no hikari', 'earthquake light') used during the Matsushiro photographic documentation.
etymology.meaning: Lights (luminous phenomena) associated in space and time with earthquakes.
etymology.first_use: Coined in scientific literature in the 1960s following the Matsushiro earthquake swarm photographs.

related_phenomena.name: Ball Lightning
related_phenomena.description: Other rare luminous atmospheric phenomenon with debated mechanism

related_phenomena.name: St. Elmo's Fire
related_phenomena.description: Plasma-based atmospheric glow on conductors in strong electric fields

related_phenomena.name: Min-Min Lights
related_phenomena.description: Anomalous nocturnal lights with debated origins

related_phenomena.name: Hessdalen Lights
related_phenomena.description: Geographically-localised anomalous lights with proposed geological mechanisms

features.description: Glowing orbs floating or moving above the ground; sometimes the most consistently photographed form
Sky-wide flashes resembling diffuse lightning, sometimes lasting fractions of a second
Vertical, flame-shaped luminous columns rising from ridges or fault traces
Diffuse low-altitude glow resembling auroral light
Lights reported up to 110 km from the epicentre (1930 Idu) and at greater distances during seismic-wave passage

physical.morphology: Optical / plasma phenomena rather than physical bodies; typical durations from milliseconds (flashes) to many seconds (orbs and columns).

abilities.description: Some reports occur seconds, hours, or days before the principal seismic event, raising hopes (and controversy) about earthquake prediction
Many reports coincide with seismic-wave arrival; modern security-camera footage (e.g. 2017 Chiapas, 2022 Michoacán) supports these accounts

habitat_preference: subvertical faults in continental rift environments
patterns: Concentrate within tens of kilometres of the epicentre for preseismic events
Appear at much greater distances during seismic-wave passage (reported 740 km from a 2017 Chiapas earthquake)
Strongly correlated with shallow continental earthquakes; less common with deep or subduction-interface events

range.description: Global; reported wherever earthquakes occur, with relatively higher report frequency in South America, China, southern Europe (Greece, Italy, France, Germany), and Japan; lower in North America.

hotspots.name: Matsushiro earthquake swarm
hotspots.notes: First well-documented photographic record of EQL, established by Yutaka Yasui and others

first_recorded.source: Japanese Sandai Jitsuroku (chronicle of the 869 Jōgan earthquake)

peak_activity_periods.description: Matsushiro swarm photographic documentation establishing scientific credibility
Widespread phone, dashcam, and security-camera footage (Peru 2007, Chile 2010, Chiapas 2017, Michoacán 2022)

origins.cultural_roots: Pre-modern records appear in Japanese, Chinese, and European chronicles; the phenomenon was widely treated as folklore or as misperception until the 1960s.
origins.earliest_accounts: 869 Jōgan earthquake (Japan) records 'strange lights in the sky'; 1888 North Canterbury (New Zealand) and 1906 San Francisco accounts are among the earliest in English-language seismology literature.
origins.folklore_connections: Pre-scientific narratives often interpreted EQL as omens, dragon breath, or atmospheric ghost-lights; modern study reframes the phenomenon as geophysical.

timeline.event: Sandai Jitsuroku records 'strange lights in the sky' coinciding with the Jōgan earthquake (Japan)
timeline.significance: Among the earliest written EQL records

timeline.event: Lights reported up to 110 km from the epicentre of the Idu earthquake (Japan)
timeline.significance: Important early modern observation of long-range EQL
timeline.source: Wikipedia, Earthquake light (citing Japanese seismological reports)

timeline.event: Beginning of the Matsushiro earthquake swarm; subsequent EQL photographs by Yutaka Yasui establish scientific credibility
timeline.source: Yasui, Y. (1968), Bulletin of the Seismological Society of America

timeline.event: J. S. Derr publishes a survey paper concluding that 'the existence of luminous phenomena…is well established'
timeline.significance: Mid-1970s mainstream acceptance of EQL existence
timeline.source: Derr, J. S. (1973), Bulletin of the Seismological Society of America

timeline.event: Friedemann Freund and colleagues publish work on positive-hole charge carriers in stressed igneous rock as a candidate EQL mechanism
timeline.significance: Establishes the dominant modern mechanistic hypothesis
timeline.source: Freund, F. T. et al. (2003), Journal of Scientific Exploration

timeline.event: Robert Thériault, Friedemann Freund, and colleagues publish 'Prevalence of Earthquake Lights Associated with Rift Environments' in 'Seismological Research Letters', surveying 65 cases since 1600
timeline.significance: Most comprehensive modern catalogue and tectonic-context analysis
timeline.source: Thériault, R. et al. (2014), Seismological Research Letters

timeline.event: Troy Shinbrot and colleagues publish a triboelectric-discharge mechanism in 'Physical Review Letters'
timeline.significance: Provides a complementary laboratory mechanism for EQL
timeline.source: Shinbrot, T. et al. (2014), Physical Review Letters

timeline.event: EQL captured on multiple security cameras during the magnitude-8.2 Chiapas earthquake (Mexico), including in Mexico City ~740 km from the epicentre
timeline.source: Mexican news media and seismological agencies

timeline.event: EQL captured during a magnitude-6.8 aftershock of the Michoacán earthquake (Mexico)
timeline.source: Mexican news media and seismological agencies

history.modern_era: EQL are now treated as a real but mechanism-uncertain geophysical phenomenon. Active research focuses on field observation networks, laboratory rock-deformation experiments, and statistical correlation between EQL reports and tectonic context.

notable.name: Matsushiro earthquake swarm EQL

types: Multiple residents and seismologists; photographic record
credibility_factors: Long swarm with extensive photographic documentation
Documented by Yutaka Yasui in peer-reviewed seismology

notable.description: During the 1965–1967 Matsushiro earthquake swarm, residents and observers photographed luminous spheres and flame-like emissions associated with earthquake events. The Yasui (1968) catalogue is considered the first photographically-documented EQL record.

credibility.notes: Foundational case for scientific acceptance of EQL.

sources: Yasui, Y. (1968), Bulletin of the Seismological Society of America

notable.name: Saguenay earthquake EQL

types: Public reports collated post-event by seismologists

notable.description: The magnitude-5.9 Saguenay earthquake of 25 November 1988 was followed by 46 documented reports of luminous phenomena, used in subsequent academic work as a benchmark modern North American case.
evidence: Citizen reports collected by seismological agencies
sources: Thériault, R. et al. (2014), Seismological Research Letters

notable.name: Chiapas earthquake EQL

location.description: Mexico City and other cities up to ~740 km from the magnitude-8.2 Chiapas epicentre

notable.description: Brief blue-white sky flashes, captured by security and dashboard cameras during seismic-wave passage from the magnitude-8.2 earthquake off Chiapas, were reported in Mexico City and elsewhere.

factors: Some footage attributable to power-grid arcing rather than EQL
Other footage unexplained by infrastructure failures
credibility.notes: Mainstream coverage acknowledged a mix of grid-arcing and apparent EQL.

photographic.name: Yasui Matsushiro photographs
photographic.description: Photographs taken during the Matsushiro earthquake swarm and published by Yutaka Yasui (1968) in the Bulletin of the Seismological Society of America.
photographic.analysis: First photographically documented EQL record; the principal source supporting mainstream scientific acceptance.

video.name: Chiapas earthquake EQL footage
video.description: Multiple security-camera and phone-camera videos showing blue-white sky flashes during the M8.2 Chiapas earthquake, with some footage explained by power-grid arcing.
video.analysis: Mix of confirmed power-grid arcing and apparent atmospheric flashes; widely cited in modern EQL discussion.

documentary.name: Prevalence of Earthquake Lights Associated with Rift Environments
documentary.description: Survey of 65 documented EQL cases since 1600 in 'Seismological Research Letters', proposing tectonic-context patterns.
documentary.analysis: Most-cited modern catalogue; supports the 'subvertical faults in rift environments' tectonic-correlation hypothesis.
documentary.source: Thériault et al. (2014), Seismological Research Letters

scientific.name: Positive-hole (p-hole) charge carriers (Freund mechanism)
scientific.description: Stressed igneous rocks containing peroxy bonds release positive-hole charge carriers under compression; these carriers travel through the rock to the surface and ionise air, producing plasma luminescence at the ground or in the lower atmosphere.
evidence_cited: Correlation of EQL with subvertical faults in rifting tectonic environments (Thériault et al. 2014)

scientific.name: Piezoelectric generation
scientific.description: Quartz-rich rocks under tectonic stress generate strong electric fields by the piezoelectric effect, occasionally sufficient to drive air breakdown and luminescence.
evidence_cited: Quartz-rich basement rock geology of many EQL-active regions

scientific.name: Triboelectric / triboluminescent discharge (Shinbrot et al. 2014)
scientific.description: Rubbing of identical rock layers under shear during fault movement generates voltage spikes and luminescence even without piezoelectric or peroxy-bond mechanisms.

scientific.name: Ionosphere / magnetic-field disturbance
scientific.description: Local disruption of Earth's magnetic field associated with seismic activity can produce ionospheric radiative recombination resembling auroral glow.
evidence_cited: Ionospheric Total Electron Content (TEC) anomalies near major earthquakes

skeptical.name: Misidentification of conventional phenomena
skeptical.description: Many alleged EQL reports are explained by lightning from concurrent storms, electrical arcing of swayed power lines, transformer flashovers, or auroral activity.
evidence_cited: Identifiable transformer arcing in multiple Chile (2010) and Chiapas (2017) videos
Coincident lightning records in some reports

traditions: Japanese chronicles describing 'strange lights' before earthquakes (Sandai Jitsuroku)
Andean and Mesoamerican narratives of luminous omens before major quakes
European mediaeval omen literature recording lights with seismic events
stories: Modern crowd-sourced testimony in Japan, Chile, and Mexico
folklore.beliefs: Pre-modern interpretations included dragon breath (East Asia), divine omens (Japan, Andes), and ghost-lights (Europe). Modern interpretation is geophysical.

literature: 'Earthquake Lights and Their Significance' — discussion in Friedemann Freund's collected work
film_tv: Featured in BBC Horizon and NHK documentary segments on earthquake science
Discussed in Discovery Channel programming on earthquake prediction
other: EQL frequently cited in earthquake-prediction popular literature, often beyond the support of current evidence

researchers.name: Friedemann T. Freund
researchers.affiliation: NASA Ames Research Center / SETI Institute / San Jose State University
researchers.contribution: Developed the positive-hole charge-carrier mechanism for EQL and pre-earthquake electromagnetic anomalies

researchers.name: Robert Thériault
researchers.affiliation: Ministère de l'Énergie et des Ressources naturelles, Quebec
researchers.contribution: Lead author on the 2014 'Seismological Research Letters' EQL prevalence survey

researchers.name: John S. Derr
researchers.contribution: Author of foundational 1973 review establishing scientific recognition of EQL

researchers.name: Yutaka Yasui
researchers.contribution: First peer-reviewed photographic documentation of EQL during the Matsushiro swarm

organizations.name: Seismological Society of America
organizations.focus: Publishes the 'Bulletin of the Seismological Society of America' and 'Seismological Research Letters'

organizations.name: Servicio Sismológico Nacional (UNAM)
organizations.focus: Mexican seismology and EQL field reports

research.ongoing_studies: Active research areas include statistical analysis of citizen-camera EQL records, laboratory replication of positive-hole and triboelectric mechanisms, ionospheric TEC monitoring in seismic regions, and field-electrode arrays at active fault zones.

sources.title: Prevalence of Earthquake Lights Associated with Rift Environments
Size-dependent particle migration and triboelectric charge segregation
Rocks That Crackle and Sparkle and Glow: Strange Pre-Earthquake Phenomena
A study on the luminous phenomena accompanied with earthquake (Part I)
sources.publication: Memoirs of the Kakioka Magnetic Observatory

sources.title: Earthquake lights: A review of observations and present theories
sources.publication: Bulletin of the Seismological Society of America
